handlers","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":184008807,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxODQwMDg4MDc=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:multithreading","name":"domain:multithreading","color":"fbca04","default":false,"description":"Base.Threads and related functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":26,"created_at":"2023-04-27T14:43:21Z","updated_at":"2024-09-30T21:35:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49541","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Interrupt handling is a tricky problem, not just in terms of implementation, but in terms of desired behavior: when an interrupt is received, which code should handle it? Julia's current answer to this is effectively to throw an `InterruptException` to the first task to hit a safepoint. While this seems sensible (the code that's running gets interrupted), it only really works for very basic numerical code. In the case that multiple tasks are running concurrently, or when try-catch handlers are registered, this system breaks down, and results in unpredictable behavior. This unpredictable behavior includes:

- Interrupting background/runtime tasks which don't want to be interrupted, as they do little bits of important work (and are critical to library runtime functionality)
- Interrupting only one task, when multiple coordinating tasks would want to receive the interrupt to safely terminate a computation
- Interrupting only one library's task, when multiple libraries really would want to be notified about the interrupt

The above behavior makes it nearly impossible to provide reliable Ctrl-C behavior, and results in very confused users who get stuck hitting Ctrl-C continuously, sometimes getting caught in a hang, sometimes triggering unrelated exception handling code they didn't mean to, sometimes getting a segfault, and very rarely getting the behavior they desire (with unpredictable safety of being able to continue using the active session as intended).

This commit provides an alternative behavior for interrupts which is more predictable: user code may now register tasks as "interrupt handlers", which will be guaranteed to receive an `InterruptException` whenever the session receives an interrupt signal. Additionally, when any interrupt handlers are registered, no other tasks will receive `InterruptException`s; only the handlers may receive them.

This behavior allows one or more libraries to register handler tasks which will all be concurrently awoken to handle each interrupt and do whatever is necessary to safely interrupt any running code; the extent to which other tasks are interrupted is library-defined. For example, GPU libraries like AMDGPU.jl can register a handler to safely interrupt GPU kernels running on all GPU queues and do resource cleanup. Concurrently, a complex runtime like the scheduler in Dagger.jl can register a handler to interrupt running tasks on other workers when possible.

Todo:
- [x] ~Consider whether to allow `force` mode to still operate with handlers registered~ Not doing this for now
- [x] ~Add `@interrupthandler` API (suggested by @Seelengrab )~ Punting as we don't have a way to register the handler during `__init__`
- [x] Add a "kill foreground task" menu option
- [x] Interrupt the foreground or root task on multi-Ctrl-C in non-REPL mode
- [x] Address missing logic in `jl_task_get_next`
- [x] Add tests
- [x] Add docs

Fixes #34184
Fixes
